chapters.title: The hidden power of emotions
chapters.text:
Whether you realize it or not, your emotions and your ability to manage them shape your success at work and at home. Emotions can lift you up at the right moment or block you from reaching your goals. They are not mysterious or out of control. They are a part of you that you can learn to manage and put to work.

To reach that level of control, you need to understand emotional intelligence, or EQ, which is a key part of your mind. By studying and developing EQ, you can learn the most important skill, which is making emotions serve you instead of letting them run your life.

We usually know what we want, but we do not always get it. The gap between where we are and where we want to be comes down to emotional intelligence.

Valeria Kozlova

Kozlova's system, called EQ-Boost, is a clear way to build and use emotional intelligence in everyday life, work, and business. The sections that follow cover the features of this system, the practical exercises behind it, and the applied details of working with EQ.

EQ-Boost gives you specific tools for mastering emotions and for using them to reach your goals faster.

Emotions show up at every step. They come with a big business decision, with the nerves before a public performance, and with the excitement of world events. You cannot avoid them, so learning to handle them well should be a priority if you want to succeed. Too many people have failed because they never thought about the role emotions play in their lives or the damage they can do.

But there is good news. You can turn strong feelings, even negative ones, from obstacles into reliable allies. The key to that shift is emotional intelligence.

chapters.title: More than a passing trend
chapters.text:
When scientists talk about emotional intelligence, they mostly mean managing feelings. When strong emotions block your access to clear thinking, EQ lets you regain control and master the situation.

Some of the most respected minds in history have spent years studying emotions. Charles Darwin, Sigmund Freud, and the American psychologists Peter Salovey and John Mayer all shaped how we think about the topic today.

The phrase "emotional intelligence" first appeared in the 1960s. The idea kept expanding across decades, moving from theory into practice. In the 1970s, more scientific work popularized the idea and showed how EQ affects productivity and memory.

In 1988, Israeli psychologist Reuven Bar-On introduced the term "emotional quotient," or EQ, to stand alongside IQ. The parallel suggests that IQ measures intellectual ability, while EQ fills in the rest of the picture.

In his well-known book Emotional Intelligence, the scientist and journalist Daniel Goleman laid out a model of EQ. That model is one of seven major frameworks and stands out for its popularity, usefulness, and practical reach.

According to Goleman, emotional intelligence has several core parts. Social skills let you communicate and connect with people. Self-awareness means understanding your own emotions, thoughts, weaknesses, and strengths. Empathy is the ability to feel with others and understand their feelings. Self-regulation is about balance and control of emotional reactions. Motivation is the drive to push toward a goal.

These parts make EQ useful in business and professional life. They matter most for leaders working with teams, because EQ improves communication and creates a positive working atmosphere. In tense moments like negotiations and interviews, EQ often decides the outcome, beating pure logic.

Relying only on rational thinking and ignoring emotions means using about half your brain. To perform at the highest level, you need a blend of rational and emotional thinking. Without EQ, reaching major business goals becomes nearly impossible.

Studies have found that people with developed EQ earn about $29,000 more per year, and about 90 percent of top managers have high EQ.

chapters.title: The stages of emotional intelligence development
chapters.text:
The good news is that EQ is not fixed. You can grow it with effort. It is a tough task, but it can be done. According to the Harvard Business Review, soft skills, which are essentially EQ, can improve by 25 percent or more after focused training and coaching.

To navigate EQ development, it helps to understand the levels of emotional intelligence. As you grow, you climb to higher levels and discover new tools for handling your feelings. At every stage, awareness of your own and other people's emotions is the key factor. People with high EQ can spot and understand feelings in themselves and in those around them, and they put those feelings to work for their benefit.

The third, or lowest, level means you can understand your own emotions and partly control them. At this level, a person is aware of feelings and can analyze them, but tends to ignore the emotions of others. People at this level often become prisoners of strong feelings they cannot handle, as if they are hiding their reactions from the rest of the world and blocking their access to rational thought.

The second level means you can manage your own emotions while also noticing and reading another person's feelings. A person at this level can hold several different emotions in mind at the same time and never becomes a prisoner of any of them. Their ability to read others helps them build effective relationships and keep communication constructive.

A person with a fairly high EQ can see others clearly, without their own emotions getting in the way.

The first, or highest, level means you can consciously manage your intuition and combine the skills of the lower levels. At this level, you can make decisions using emotions, relying on gut feelings. You can also keep many tasks in your head at once without losing focus.

The first level has two sub-levels. Sub-level A is when emotions help you decide without any rational basis. Sub-level B is when you use intuition in a controlled way to decide.

A person at the top level of EQ can succeed even in uncertain conditions, because they can switch easily between emotional and rational approaches and pick the one that fits the moment.

chapters.title: Introducing EQ-Boost Technology
chapters.text:
Valeria Kozlova's EQ-Boost system grew out of 20 years of practice, study, and refinement through real client work. EQ-Boost is a set of practical tools that anyone can use to apply emotional intelligence in any area of life.

EQ-Boost took shape by studying how the brain works and was tested through experiments. The system uses carefully designed language models that guide the brain in useful directions.

Balancing the rational and the emotional brings the highest efficiency, which is why EQ-Boost uses the formula EQ plus IQ equals result.

To make the system easier to apply, Kozlova highlights three core axioms. First, emotions always start inside you, and there is no way to avoid that. Second, every emotion goes through three stages, which are generation, realization, and expression. Third, you can reach peak efficiency only when you fully control your body and understand your feelings.

Emotions are your responses to outside and inside triggers. Generating an emotion is only the first step. Recognition and understanding come next. Only by working through this process can you reach clear-headed decision-making. It lets you make rational and objective choices without being pushed around by emotion. In short, realizing the emotion lets you manage your reactions and use EQ instead of acting on primal instincts.

The expression stage happens when you put the emotion into words, for yourself or for others. Instead of bottling up feelings, it helps to let them out in a constructive way. Unacknowledged feelings always leak out through facial expressions, body language, and appearance.

EQ-Boost says it is vital to put into words what is going on inside you. Until you do, you wander through a maze of tangled thoughts and desires, and neither you nor anyone else fully understands you.

Valeria Kozlova

EQ-Boost focuses on merging external and internal language, that is, what you hear from others with your own words and thoughts. Synchronizing these two languages is a key step toward success. The External and Internal Speech technique can help. Take a piece of paper and divide it into two columns. The first is for external language, the second for internal. Verbally express any intention in the form "I am doing..." and write it in the first column. Then listen for the words of your inner voice that come up as a reaction, and write those in the second column. Compare the two columns.

For example, when you say out loud "I am quitting my job," your inner voice may support or contradict you with "You can't do that." By syncing the two voices inside you, you can move toward your goal with confidence and clarity.

chapters.title: How to use EQ-Boost
chapters.text:
EQ-Boost offers a simple algorithm for working with emotions. The algorithm has four steps that you follow in order.

First, ask yourself what you are feeling right now. Second, say the name of the emotion out loud. Third, ask yourself what thought or word triggered that feeling. Fourth, find the link between the thought and the feeling and say it out loud.

When a person spots the link between an emotion and the thought that caused it, they return quickly to a productive state and their mood improves. Finding the right links can be hard, especially with complex emotions, but it is the key to raising EQ.

By working through emotions in this order, you stop them from draining your productivity and open up new possibilities for your brain. You find the right words faster, see new ways to solve problems, and understand the world around you better.

Continuous contact and attention to your emotions is the main goal of EQ-Boost, because that is how EQ grows and gets applied.

Another key part of EQ-Boost is building mental resources. Everyone has a limited amount. People can only handle a certain number of events and emotions at once. Burnout and apathy show up when that inner cup overflows.

Painful childhood memories and other emotional events slowly drain mental resources, so finding more resources becomes a real challenge. Some people turn to destructive habits like alcohol, nicotine, or pills. Others turn to therapy, mentorship, or the support of loved ones. EQ-Boost suggests a different source of additional resources, which is awareness of emotions.

Valeria Kozlova points out that unconscious emotions use up far more mental resources than conscious and meaningful ones. Identifying and structuring them can free up about 30 percent of extra cognitive capacity. Managing this well is a top priority of EQ.

Mental resources are replenishable, and the Thailand Technique, which is part of EQ-Boost, can help. Like a vacation in sunny Thailand, it can become a source of support, a stress antidote, and a source of emotional strength. The algorithm has five steps.

First, list all the sources of tension in your life right now, including events and problems that drain your energy and trigger strong feelings, both positive and negative. Second, opposite each source of tension, write the emotions it triggers. Third, in a third column, rate the strength of each emotion from 1 to 10. Fourth, use EQ-Boost by naming the emotions out loud and connecting them with the thoughts that triggered them. Fifth, say out loud all the thought-emotion links you notice and re-rate the strength of those emotions. If the intensity has dropped, you have successfully identified the tension, the emotion, and the thought, and they will no longer cause discomfort.

It is important to work through each source of tension one at a time and only move on when you feel relief from the last one.

The Thailand Technique is quick and useful, but it takes steady practice to sharpen the skill of spotting links between emotions and thoughts.

chapters.title: Emotional intelligence as a guide to the world
chapters.text:
Rational thinking has long been treated as the peak of efficiency in business and work, while emotions have been seen as obstacles to balanced decisions. In fact, the emotional way of thinking is just as essential and useful as the rational one, because emotions can sharpen decisions and speed them up. People with high EQ can switch between the two systems and get the best of each.

EQ-Boost encourages and teaches you to blend the two systems into a third level, the rationally-emotional level, which makes you more effective.

Trusting the emotional system is usually harder than trusting the rational one, because it always feels risky and a bit like working for the future. Emotional people keep chasing a bird in the sky, while rational people settle for a feather in their hand. Blending the two styles gives unexpected advantages. Emotional people learn to manage their feelings, and rational people gain structure.

When using EQ-Boost, it is also important to remember the risks and limits. One risk is mental traps. While analyzing thoughts and feelings, we often notice links that look correct at first glance but are actually false. These mental traps are attempts by the brain to dress up unconscious emotions in rational clothing.

Mental traps show up most often under fear and uncertainty. A man who wants to meet a woman he finds attractive might talk himself out of it by saying she is "not his type." That sounds logical on the surface, but it is a mental trap hiding the real emotion, which is the fear of rejection. Examples like this are everywhere, and all of them are dangerous because they block change and success.

There are also cases where emotional intelligence does not help and will not lead to the success you want in your career or business. This usually happens because of beliefs and limits imposed by the state, religion, or another community. These long-held beliefs get passed down through generations and stop people from acting differently, which makes the full EQ toolkit hard to use. EQ-Boost can help break free from that dependent model by gradually teaching you to separate your own thoughts and feelings from those that were imposed on you.

chapters.title: Wrapping up
chapters.text:
The benefits of high emotional intelligence could fill volumes. Your EQ supports healthy relationships, constructive communication, a life with less stress, and success at home and at work.

By studying your emotions and your limits, you open the door to new possibilities and move closer to a better life, one not weighed down by unconscious emotions, mental traps, or emotional breakdowns.

EQ-Boost is a hands-on algorithm that you can apply right away to organize your emotional inner world and free up precious mental capacity. Now that you have a foundation in EQ and EQ-Boost, the next step is yours. Dare to take the first independent step toward developing your EQ and start using the techniques and ideas here in real life. You can only understand the details of working with emotions and thoughts by applying EQ-Boost in real situations.

If you focus on the main idea of EQ-Boost, which is the link between thoughts and emotions, you can turn it into a habit that does not require extra effort but keeps delivering real benefits. So, what are you waiting for?

Try to spot the emotion you are feeling right now, name it, and find the thought that triggered it. Use this algorithm whenever you can so that analyzing emotions, thoughts, and their connections becomes second nature. Watch the video interview with Valeria Kozlova to explore the widest horizons of EQ ideas and their value.
